08 Indexes & Performance — Making Queries Fast

← Back to lessons

Knowing SQL is one thing. Knowing fast SQL is another. In a newsroom database with millions of rows, the difference between 30 seconds and 30 milliseconds is the difference between a useful tool and an unusable one. Indexes are how you get from 30 seconds to 30 milliseconds.

The Mental Model — Book Index Analogy

Imagine you have a 500-page textbook and you need to find every mention of "PostgreSQL." You have two options:

  • Without an index: Read every page, start to finish, scanning for the word. This is a sequential scan (or "full table scan"). It works, but it's slow.
  • With an index: Flip to the index at the back, look up "PostgreSQL," and go directly to pages 12, 47, 189, 301. Done in seconds.

A database index works exactly the same way. It's a separate data structure that maps values to their row locations, so the database can jump directly to what it needs instead of scanning every row.

The tradeoff is the same as a book index too. The index takes up extra pages (storage space). Every time the book is updated, the index needs updating too (write performance). But for lookups, it's dramatically faster. Worth it for columns you search frequently; wasteful for columns you rarely query.

How B-Tree Indexes Work

The default index type in PostgreSQL is a B-tree (balanced tree). You don't need to understand the full data structure, but the mental model helps.

Think of a B-tree like a phone book organized alphabetically. To find "Martinez":

  1. Open to the middle of the book — you're at "K." Too early.
  2. Open to the middle of the second half — you're at "P." Too late.
  3. Open to the middle of K-P — you're at "M." Close.
  4. A few more steps and you're at "Martinez."

Each step cuts the remaining possibilities in half. For a table with 1 million rows, a B-tree index finds any value in about 20 steps (log base 2 of 1,000,000). Without an index, it might need to check all 1,000,000 rows.

Table SizeWithout Index (Seq Scan)With B-tree Index
1,000 rows1,000 checks~10 steps
100,000 rows100,000 checks~17 steps
1,000,000 rows1,000,000 checks~20 steps
10,000,000 rows10,000,000 checks~23 steps

B-trees handle more than equality. Because values are sorted, B-tree indexes work for =, <, >, <=, >=, BETWEEN, IN, LIKE 'prefix%', and ORDER BY. They do NOT help with LIKE '%suffix' (pattern doesn't start with a fixed prefix).

Creating and Dropping Indexes

Basic syntax

-- Create an index on a single column
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_author_id
ON articles (author_id);

-- Create an index with a custom name
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_published_date
ON articles (published_date);

-- Drop an index
DROP INDEX idx_articles_author_id;

The naming convention is idx_tablename_columnname. It's not required, but it makes your indexes self-documenting.

Unique indexes

-- Unique index: enforces uniqueness + speeds up lookups
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email
ON users (email);

-- This is essentially the same as adding a UNIQUE constraint
-- but you get both the constraint and the performance benefit
Prisma schema
model User {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  email String @unique
  name  String

  @@index([name])
}

model Article {
  id        Int @id @default(autoincrement())
  authorId  Int
  section   String
  published Boolean

  @@index([authorId])
  @@index([section, published])
}
SQL equivalent
-- @unique creates a unique index
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email
ON users (email);

-- @@index creates a regular index
CREATE INDEX idx_users_name
ON users (name);


CREATE INDEX idx_articles_author_id
ON articles (author_id);

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_published
ON articles (section, published);

Prisma creates indexes for you. When you add @unique or @@index in your schema and run prisma migrate dev, it generates CREATE INDEX SQL. Now you know what that migration is actually doing — and you can add indexes manually when you need to optimize queries Prisma doesn't know about.

What's already indexed?

PostgreSQL automatically creates indexes for:

  • Primary keys — every @id column gets a unique B-tree index
  • Unique constraints — every @unique column gets a unique B-tree index

You do NOT get automatic indexes on foreign keys. This is a common performance trap — joins on unindexed foreign keys are slow.

-- Check what indexes exist on a table
SELECT indexname, indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'articles';

Composite Indexes — Column Order Matters

A composite index covers multiple columns. The column order is critical because B-trees sort left to right.

-- Composite index on (section, published_date)
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_date
ON articles (section, published_date);

Think of it like a phone book sorted by last name, then first name. You can efficiently look up:

  • Everyone named "Smith" (first column only) — works
  • Everyone named "Smith, John" (both columns) — works
  • Everyone named "John" regardless of last name (second column only) — does NOT work efficiently
-- This composite index: (section, published_date)
-- HELPS these queries:
WHERE section = 'news'                                  -- uses first column
WHERE section = 'news' AND published_date > '2024-01-01' -- uses both columns
WHERE section = 'news' ORDER BY published_date           -- uses both columns

-- DOES NOT HELP this query:
WHERE published_date > '2024-01-01'   -- skips the first column!

The leftmost prefix rule: A composite index on (A, B, C) can be used for queries on (A), (A, B), or (A, B, C). But NOT for (B), (C), or (B, C) alone. Always put the most frequently filtered column first.

Choosing column order

Put the column with higher selectivity (more distinct values) first, especially if you filter on it with =. Then add columns used for range queries or sorting:

-- Good: filter by section (=), then sort by date
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_date
ON articles (section, published_date DESC);

-- Good: filter by author (=), then by published status (=), then sort
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_author_pub_date
ON articles (author_id, published, published_date DESC);

-- The DESC in the index definition matches DESC queries efficiently

EXPLAIN and EXPLAIN ANALYZE

How do you know if your query is using an index? EXPLAIN shows you the database's plan before running the query. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs it and shows real timing.

EXPLAIN — the query plan

EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM articles WHERE author_id = 5;

-- Without an index on author_id:
-- Seq Scan on articles  (cost=0.00..1250.00 rows=50 width=200)
--   Filter: (author_id = 5)

-- With an index on author_id:
-- Index Scan using idx_articles_author_id on articles  (cost=0.29..8.31 rows=50 width=200)
--   Index Cond: (author_id = 5)

Key things to look for:

Scan TypeMeaningSpeed
Seq ScanReading every row in the tableSlow on large tables
Index ScanUsing an index to find specific rowsFast
Index Only ScanGetting all data from the index itselfFastest
Bitmap Index ScanUsing index to build a map, then fetching rowsGood for many matches

EXPLAIN ANALYZE — real execution time

EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM articles WHERE author_id = 5;

-- Index Scan using idx_articles_author_id on articles
--   (cost=0.29..8.31 rows=50 width=200)
--   (actual time=0.015..0.089 rows=47 loops=1)
--   Index Cond: (author_id = 5)
-- Planning Time: 0.085 ms
-- Execution Time: 0.112 ms

The key numbers:

  • actual time — how long the scan took (in milliseconds)
  • rows — how many rows were returned (compare to rows=50 estimate)
  • Planning Time — time to figure out the plan
  • Execution Time — total time to run the query

EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes the query. If it's a DELETE or UPDATE, wrap it in a transaction you roll back: BEGIN; EXPLAIN ANALYZE DELETE FROM ...; ROLLBACK;

Reading a complex query plan

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT a.title, u.name
FROM articles a
JOIN users u ON a.author_id = u.id
WHERE a.section = 'news' AND a.published = true
ORDER BY a.published_date DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Limit  (actual time=0.050..0.085 rows=10)
--   -> Sort  (actual time=0.049..0.052 rows=10)
--         Sort Key: a.published_date DESC
--         -> Nested Loop  (actual time=0.020..0.040 rows=42)
--               -> Index Scan using idx_articles_section_pub on articles a
--                     (actual time=0.010..0.018 rows=42)
--                     Index Cond: (section = 'news' AND published = true)
--               -> Index Scan using users_pkey on users u
--                     (actual time=0.001..0.001 rows=1)
--                     Index Cond: (id = a.author_id)

Read the plan from the inside out (most indented first). Here the database:

  1. Used an index to find articles matching section='news' AND published=true (42 rows)
  2. For each of those 42 rows, looked up the user by primary key (nested loop join)
  3. Sorted the results by published_date DESC
  4. Returned only the first 10

Index-Only Scans — The Fastest Reads

An index-only scan happens when all the columns you need are IN the index itself, so the database never has to read the actual table rows. It's the fastest possible read.

-- Create an index that covers section and published_date
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_date
ON articles (section, published_date);

-- This query can be answered entirely from the index
EXPLAIN SELECT section, published_date
FROM articles
WHERE section = 'news';
-- Index Only Scan using idx_articles_section_date on articles
--   Index Cond: (section = 'news')

If your query also selects title or views (columns not in the index), it falls back to a regular Index Scan, which still has to read from the table.

Covering indexes with INCLUDE

PostgreSQL lets you add extra columns to an index that aren't used for sorting but are available for index-only scans:

-- INCLUDE adds columns to the index leaf pages
-- They're available for index-only scans but not for filtering
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_covering
ON articles (section, published_date)
INCLUDE (title, views);

-- Now this query can use an index-only scan
SELECT section, published_date, title, views
FROM articles
WHERE section = 'news'
ORDER BY published_date DESC;

Don't go overboard with INCLUDE. Every column you add makes the index larger, which means more storage and slower writes. Only include columns that appear in your most critical, most-run queries.

When NOT to Index

Indexes aren't free. Every index you add has costs:

  • Storage space: Each index is a separate data structure on disk
  • Write speed: Every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must update ALL indexes on that table
  • Planning time: More indexes = more options for the query planner to evaluate

Here's when indexes are a bad idea:

SituationWhy an Index Hurts
Small tables (<1,000 rows)Sequential scan is already fast enough. The overhead of using an index might actually be slower.
Columns with low cardinalityA boolean column has only 2 values. An index won't narrow things down much.
Write-heavy tables with rare readsLogs, events, audit trails — you're paying the write cost on every insert but rarely querying.
Columns you never filter/sort onAn index on bio or description is wasteful if you never use them in WHERE or ORDER BY.
Frequently updated columnsEvery update to the column means updating the index too. The cost can outweigh the benefit.

Rule of thumb: Index columns that appear in WHERE, JOIN ON, and ORDER BY clauses of your most frequent queries. Don't index everything "just in case." Start with no indexes (besides the automatic ones on primary keys and unique constraints), then add them when EXPLAIN shows a problem.

Partial and Expression Indexes

Partial indexes

A partial index only indexes rows that match a condition. If you frequently query only published articles, why index the drafts?

-- Only index published articles
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_published_only
ON articles (published_date DESC)
WHERE published = true;

-- This query uses the partial index
SELECT * FROM articles
WHERE published = true
ORDER BY published_date DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- This query CANNOT use it (doesn't match the WHERE condition)
SELECT * FROM articles
WHERE published = false;

Partial indexes are smaller and faster because they contain fewer rows. They're perfect when you have a common filter that eliminates most of the table.

Expression indexes

You can index the result of an expression or function:

-- Index on lowercase email (for case-insensitive lookups)
CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_lower
ON users (LOWER(email));

-- This query can now use the index
SELECT * FROM users
WHERE LOWER(email) = 'sarah@newsroom.com';

-- Index on year extracted from a date
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_year
ON articles (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM published_date));

-- This query uses it
SELECT * FROM articles
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM published_date) = 2024;

The expression in the query must match the expression in the index exactly. If your index is on LOWER(email), your WHERE clause must use LOWER(email), not email or UPPER(email).

Prisma @@index vs Raw CREATE INDEX

Prisma's @@index covers the basics. But raw SQL gives you more options:

FeaturePrisma @@indexRaw CREATE INDEX
Basic single-column indexYesYes
Composite indexYesYes
Unique index@@unique / @uniqueCREATE UNIQUE INDEX
Partial index (WHERE)NoYes
Expression indexNoYes
INCLUDE columnsNoYes
Sort order (ASC/DESC)LimitedYes
GIN/GiST index typestype: parameterYes

When Prisma's built-in options aren't enough, you can add raw SQL in your migrations:

// In prisma/migrations/add_partial_index/migration.sql
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_published_recent
ON articles (published_date DESC)
WHERE published = true;

CREATE INDEX idx_users_email_lower
ON users (LOWER(email));

Practical: Optimizing Slow Newsroom Queries

Let's walk through a real optimization scenario. You have a newsroom CMS with these tables and a query that's running slow.

-- The slow query: "Show me the latest 20 published articles in the 'news' section"
-- Table has 500,000 articles
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT a.title, a.published_date, u.name AS author
FROM articles a
JOIN users u ON a.author_id = u.id
WHERE a.section = 'news'
  AND a.published = true
ORDER BY a.published_date DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Result WITHOUT indexes:
-- Seq Scan on articles  (actual time=0.015..1847.230 rows=20)
-- Execution Time: 1847.582 ms   <-- almost 2 seconds!

Step by step optimization:

-- Step 1: Add an index on the WHERE columns
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_pub
ON articles (section, published);

-- Execution Time: 245.102 ms  (7x faster, but still slow)

-- Step 2: Add published_date to the index for sorting
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_pub_date
ON articles (section, published, published_date DESC);

-- Execution Time: 0.892 ms  (2000x faster than the original!)

-- Step 3: Make sure the JOIN column is indexed
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_author_id
ON articles (author_id);

-- Execution Time: 0.341 ms  (final result)

-- Step 4 (optional): Add a partial index for even better performance
DROP INDEX idx_articles_section_pub_date;
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_published_news
ON articles (published_date DESC)
WHERE section = 'news' AND published = true;

-- Execution Time: 0.125 ms  (the partial index is even smaller)

From 1.8 seconds to 0.1 milliseconds. That's a 14,000x improvement from adding the right indexes. This is why understanding indexes matters — it's the difference between a page that loads instantly and one that times out.

Quick Check

1. What's the book analogy for a database index?

An index at the back of a book. Instead of reading every page (sequential scan), you look up the term in the index and jump directly to the right pages (index scan). It takes extra space and needs updating when the book changes, but makes lookups dramatically faster.

2. You have a composite index on (section, published_date). Will WHERE published_date = '2024-01-01' use the index?

No. A composite index follows the leftmost prefix rule. It can be used for queries on (section) or (section, published_date), but NOT (published_date) alone because it skips the first column.

3. What's the difference between EXPLAIN and EXPLAIN ANALYZE?

EXPLAIN shows the planned execution strategy without running the query. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs the query and shows real timing data. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE for accurate performance measurement, but be careful with DELETE/UPDATE (wrap in a transaction and ROLLBACK).

4. Why shouldn't you index a boolean column like published by itself?

Boolean columns have only 2 distinct values (low cardinality). An index can only narrow results to roughly half the table, which often isn't selective enough for the index to be worthwhile. The database might still choose a sequential scan. However, booleans work well as PART of a composite index or in a partial index condition.

5. What does Prisma's @@index generate under the hood?

A CREATE INDEX SQL statement in the migration file. @@index([authorId]) becomes CREATE INDEX idx_... ON table (author_id). Prisma handles basic indexes but can't create partial indexes, expression indexes, or covering indexes — you need raw SQL for those.

Practice Exercise

You have a table articles (500K rows) with columns: id, title, author_id, section, published, views, published_date. Currently only the primary key id has an index.

A: A query WHERE author_id = 42 is slow. Write the index to fix it.

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_author_id
ON articles (author_id);

B: The newsroom's homepage runs WHERE section = 'news' AND published = true ORDER BY published_date DESC LIMIT 10. Design the optimal index.

-- Option 1: Composite index
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_section_pub_date
ON articles (section, published, published_date DESC);

-- Option 2: Partial index (even better for this specific query)
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_news_published
ON articles (published_date DESC)
WHERE section = 'news' AND published = true;

C: Users search for articles by title using WHERE LOWER(title) LIKE 'breaking%'. Write an expression index that helps.

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_title_lower
ON articles (LOWER(title) varchar_pattern_ops);
-- varchar_pattern_ops enables prefix pattern matching with LIKE

D: How would you check if your new index is actually being used?

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT * FROM articles
WHERE section = 'news' AND published = true
ORDER BY published_date DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Look for "Index Scan" or "Index Only Scan" in the output
-- If you see "Seq Scan", the index isn't being used

E: Write the Prisma schema equivalent for indexing author_id and a composite index on (section, published).

model Article {
  id            Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  title         String
  authorId      Int
  section       String
  published     Boolean
  views         Int
  publishedDate DateTime

  @@index([authorId])
  @@index([section, published])
}

Cheat Sheet

-- Create an index
CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table (column);
CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table (col1, col2);     -- composite
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_name ON table (column);  -- unique

-- Drop an index
DROP INDEX idx_name;

-- Partial index (only index rows matching condition)
CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table (column)
WHERE condition = true;

-- Expression index
CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table (LOWER(column));

-- Covering index (include extra columns for index-only scans)
CREATE INDEX idx_name ON table (col1)
INCLUDE (col2, col3);

-- Check existing indexes
SELECT indexname, indexdef
FROM pg_indexes
WHERE tablename = 'your_table';

-- Analyze query performance
EXPLAIN SELECT ...;             -- show the plan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ...;     -- run it and show timing

-- Safely analyze destructive queries
BEGIN;
EXPLAIN ANALYZE DELETE FROM ...;
ROLLBACK;

-- Prisma equivalents
-- @unique        = CREATE UNIQUE INDEX
-- @@index([col]) = CREATE INDEX
-- Raw SQL in migrations for partial/expression indexes
← Lesson 07: Data Types & Constraints Lesson 09: PostgreSQL-Specific Features →
